preFix.title: Carved cherry tasting spoon
preFix.subtitle: A small green-cherry spoon for tasting from the pot
preFix.excerpt: A short-handled tasting spoon in green cherry. Small enough to keep beside the hob, food-safe when finished with raw linseed oil. Cherry carves cleanly and takes a smooth oil finish.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the small-treen shaping method; Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for kitchen-utensil proportions.

content.text: A tasting spoon lives beside the hob: short handle so it fits a small ramekin or spice dish, shallow bowl so a single mouthful of soup or sauce goes in cleanly. Cherry carves well
green
and dries food-safe without tannin staining. The small size means the whole project takes two to three hours of hand-carving.
Wood
Cherry is the recommendation: fine grain, carves cleanly across the grain when green, dries to a warm pinkish-brown that darkens with age and oil. The starting
blank
needs to be only 16 cm long and 4 cm wide. A branch pruning or coppice off-cut at that size is ideal. Sycamore is the alternative: dries equally food-safe, slightly softer to carve.
Tools
Sloyd knife: for handle shaping and the back of the bowl.
Hook knife, small: for hollowing. The tight radius suits the small bowl of a tasting spoon.
Leather strop: strop every fifteen minutes.
Raw linseed oil for finishing.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
Shaping the blank

Split the branch section along the pith. Remove the pith side completely; working with pith in the blank risks a split down the centre as the wood dries. You are now working from a half-round piece. Draw the spoon shape on the flat face with pencil: 10 cm handle, 6 cm bowl section.
Use
push cuts

along the handle sides to bring the profile in to the drawn line. The blade travels away from your body, off the end of the blank. Cherry green is soft enough that you do not need the carving axe on a piece this small; the sloyd knife alone is sufficient.
The back of the bowl

With the flat face down, work the back of the bowl to a shallow dome. Hold the blank in the four-finger grip, fingers curled below. Take slicing push cuts from the bowl tip toward the throat, blade angled at about 30 degrees to the long axis. Cherry grain runs cleanly end-to-end; cuts from tip to throat go with the grain and lift a thin curl. Cuts in the reverse direction will tear; if the surface starts to roughen, you have turned the cut around.
Hollowing the bowl

Switch to the small hook knife. The tasting-spoon bowl needs to be only 5 to 6 mm deep and about 30 mm wide at the rim. Work from the rim toward the centre in short arcs. Keep the hook moving; stalling the cut in one spot will burnish rather than cut. Wall thickness is 2 mm; the bowl is small enough that you can judge by eye rather than by feel.
Finishing

Air-dry for one day; the small piece dries faster than a large ladle. Apply a thin coat of raw linseed oil, wipe off the surplus after one hour, and repeat once. Allow two weeks to cure fully before food contact.
Variations
Honey dipper end: carve the handle tip to a small bulb for hanging on a honey jar rim.
Kolrosing decoration: once fully dry and oiled, cut fine lines into the handle with the sloyd knife and rub in ground coffee for a dark pattern.
Left-handed bowl: reverse the hook-knife arc so the hollow tilts to the left for a left-handed grip.
Care
Hand-wash, never dishwasher. Towel-dry immediately. Re-oil every two months or when the wood looks dry.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Dry at room temperature away from direct heat. A crack on a small tasting spoon can be stabilised by working a drop of raw linseed into it with a fingertip and allowing it to cure.
attrs.cause: Dried too fast or wall too thin at one point
attrs.symptom: The bowl wall cracked during drying

attrs.fix: On a piece this small, 6 mm is the minimum throat thickness. Once snapped, the two halves can be fitted together and used as a display piece; carve a fresh spoon with a stronger throat.
attrs.symptom: The handle snapped at the throat

attrs.fix: Strop the hook knife and try a stroke in the opposite direction. Cherry is fine-grained but the grain still has a direction; torn fibres mean you are working uphill.
attrs.cause: Hook knife dull or cutting against grain
attrs.symptom: The bowl surface looks torn rather than cut

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
